Notes: R_Programming- Introduction to R Programming

Introduction to R Programming
  1. ​​​​​​​R is a powerful, open-source programming language and software environment for statistical computing, data analysis, and graphics.
  2. It was created by Ross Ihaka and Robert Gentleman at the University of Auckland, New Zealand, in 1993.
  3. Since then, it has become one of the most popular languages for data analysis and statistical computing, used by statisticians, data scientists, and researchers around the world.
  4. R has a large, active user community that has created a vast number of packages for data analysis, visualization, and machine learning. 
  5. This makes it possible to perform complex data analysis and modeling tasks with just a few lines of code.
  6. Additionally, R has a rich set of tools for visualizing and presenting data, which makes it ideal for exploratory data analysis and presenting results.
​
why R
  1. It is a great resource for data analysis, data visualization, data science and machine learning
  2. It provides many statistical techniques (such as statistical tests, classification, clustering and data reduction)
  3. It is easy to draw graphs in R, like pie charts, histograms, box plot, scatter plot, etc++
  4. It works on different platforms (Windows, Mac, Linux)
  5. It is open-source and free
  6. It has a large community support
  7. It has many packages (libraries of functions) that can be used to solve different problems.
​
image widget
Installation of R
R programming is a very popular language and to work on that we have to install two things, i.e., R and RStudio. R and RStudio works together to create a project on R.

The official site https://cloud.r-project.org
provides binary files for major operating systems including Windows, Linux, and Mac OS. In some Linux distributions, R is installed by default, which we can verify from the console by entering R.

Install R in Windows
There are following steps used to install the R in Windows:

Step 1:
First, we have to download the R setup from https://cloud.r-project.org/bin/windows/base/
.
Step 2:
When we click on Download R-4.2.1 for Windows, our downloading will be started of R setup.
Once the downloading is finished, we have to run the setup of R in the following way:
  1. Select the path where we want to download the R and proceed to Next.
  2. Select all components which we want to install, and then we will proceed to Next.
  3. In the next step, we have to select  accept the default, and then we proceed to Next.
  4. When we proceed to next, our installation of R in our system will get started.
  5. In the last, we will click on finish to successfully install R in our system.
Difference Between Python and R

FeatureRPython
IntroductionR is a language and environment for statistical programming which includes statistical computing and graphics.Python is a general purpose programming language for data analysis and scientific computing
ObjectiveIt has many features which are useful for statistical analysis and representation.It can be used to develop GUI applications and web applications as well as with embedded systems
WorkabilityIt has many easy to use packages for performing tasksIt can easily perform matrix computation as well as optimization
Integrated development environmentVarious popular R IDEs are Rstudio, RKward, R commander, etc.Various popular Python IDEs are Spyder, Eclipse+Pydev, Atom, etc.
Libraries and packagesThere are many packages and libraries like ggplot2, caret, etc.Some essential packages and libraries are Pandas, Numpy, Scipy, etc.
ScopeIt is mainly used for complex data analysis in data science.It takes a more streamlined approach for data science projects.
Finding Help
Getting help on a specific function
To read more about a given function, for example mean, the R function help() can be used as follow:

help(mean)

Or use this:
​​​​​​​
?mean

​​​​​​​?mean Get help of a particular function.
 help.search(‘weighted mean’) Search the help files for a word or phrase. 
help(package = ‘dplyr’) Find help for a package. 
Code Editor for R
​​​​​​​R can be run in the command line for terminal and graphical user interfaces in integrated development environments. Following are the some of the IDE used for r programming.
  1. Rstudio
  2. Visual Studio Code.
  3. Sublime Text.
  4. Notepad++
  5. Atom.
  6. Brackets.
  7. UltraEdit.
  8. TextEdit.
  9. Edit+
Exploring r GUI
​​​​​​​R Studio is an integrated development environment(IDE) for R. IDE is a GUI.
  1. R Studio is available as both Open source and Commercial software.
  2. R Studio is also available as both Desktop and Server versions.
  3. R Studio is also available for various platforms such as Windows, Linux, and macOS.
  4. Introduction to R studio for beginners:
  5. Rstudio is an open-source tool that provides Ide to use R language, and enterprise-ready professional software for data science teams to develop share the work with their team.
  6. R Studio can be downloaded from its official Website (https://rstudio.com/)

​​​​​​​R Studio:
  1. The console panel(left panel) is the place where R is waiting for you to tell it what to do, and see the results that are generated when you type in the commands.
  2. To the top right, you have the Environmental/History panel. It contains 2 tabs:
    1. Environment tab: It shows the variables that are generated during the course of programming in a workspace that is temporary.
    2. History tab: In this tab, you’ll see all the commands that are used till now from the start of usage of R Studio.
  3. To the right bottom, you have another panel, which contains multiple tabs, such as files, plots, packages, help, and viewer.
    1. The Files tab shows the files and directories that are available within the default workspace of R.
    2. The Plots tab shows the plots that are generated during the course of programming.
    3. The Packages tab helps you to look at what are the packages that are already installed in the R Studio and it also gives a user interface to install new packages.
    4. The Help tab is the most important one where you can get help from the R Documentation on the functions that are in built-in R.
    5. The final and last tab is that the Viewer tab which can be used to see the local web content that’s generated using R.

      ​
      ​​​​​​​
      ​
Exploring R studio
​​​​​​​Set the working directory in R Studio
R is always pointed at a directory on our computer.
We can find out which directory by running the getwd() function.
Note: this function has no arguments.
We can set the working directory manually in two ways:
  1. The first way is to use the console and using the command setwd(“directorypath”).
    You can use this function setwd() and give the path of the directory which you want to be the working directory for R studio, in the double codes.
  2. The second way is to set the working directory from the GUI.
    • To set the working directory from the GUI you have to click on this 3 dots button. When you click this, this will open up a file browser, which will help you to choose your working directory.
    • Once you choose your working directory, you need to use this setting button in the more tab and click it and then you get a popup menu, where you need to select “Set as working directory”.
    • This will select the current directory, which you have chosen using this file browser as your working directory. Once you set the working directory, you are ready to program in R Studio.

Create an RStudio project
Step 1: Select the FILE option and select create option.
Step 2: Then select the New Project option.
Step 3: Then choose the path and directory name.
Finally, project are created in a specific location:

Creating your first R script
  1. Create a new script: To create a new script in R Studio, click on the "File" menu, then select "New File" and "R Script".
  2. Write your code: In the newly created script, you can start writing your R code. For example, you can start by printing a simple message:
    # This is a comment in R
    # To print a message, use the print() function
    print("Hello, World!")
  3. Save the script: After you have written your code, save the script by clicking on the "File" menu and selecting "Save" or "Save As".
  4. Run the script: To run the script, you can either click the "Run" button in the script editor or use the keyboard shortcut Ctrl + Enter (Windows) or Cmd + Enter (Mac). The output of your code will be displayed in the R console below the script editor.
Navigating directories in R studio
  1. getwd(): Returns the current working directory.
  2. setwd(): Set the working directory.
  3. dir(): Return the list of the directory.
  4. sessionInfo(): Return the session of the windows.
  5. date(): Return the current date.
Workspace
​​​​​​​R workspace is your current R working environment and includes all the objects and functions you have created.
The user can anytime save an image of the current workspace that is automatically reloaded the next time R is started.

Workspace Objects
Viewing list of objects in workspace
>ls()

Saving workspace with name “first”
>save.image("D:\\first")

Loading workspace with name “first”
>load("D:\\first")
